channel.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India
channel.description: World News Today: Read the latest world news headlines, Current International breaking news world wide. In depth analysis and top news headlines world wide.

image.title: World News, Today World News, Latest International News, World Breaking News, Trending News of World - Times of India

item.title: Indian man dies in skydiving accident in Massachusetts, fell from 30 feet above in 'sudden, unexpected gust of wind'
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Meet Mira Trivedi, Indian-origin student in New Jersey who's pushing her school to not call Hitler's symbol 'swastika'
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Britain bought 1,525 acres nearly lifeless farmland, now beavers are helping it make money
item.description: A farm in Lincolnshire is evolving into a lucrative wildland endeavor, with beavers playing a pivotal role in this ecological project. The initiative focuses on demonstrating that restoring nature can lead to substantial private income. Current revenue streams are generated through carbon removal and biodiversity credits, with aspirations to draw significant investments for sustainable environmental solutions.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Meet Carol Ruckdeschel: 84-year-old 'wildest woman in America' who has been living on an island for the 53 years in order to preserve nature for the future
item.description: Carol Ruckdeschel has lived on Cumberland Island for 53 years, preserving its natural state. She meticulously documents the island's diverse flora and fauna in her field journals. Her dedication led to legislative changes protecting sea turtles from shrimping trawlers. Ruckdeschel sustains herself by living off the land and off the grid. She continues to advocate for the island's preservation against development proposals.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Two brothers detained by Pakistani forces found dead in Balochistan; custodial death concerns raised
item.description: Two brothers, Imam and Muhammad Umar, were found dead with bullet injuries in Balochistan. They were allegedly taken into custody by security personnel after their motorcycle ran out of fuel. Their family later received their bodies, disputing claims of militant links. Local residents expressed concern over the circumstances surrounding their deaths. No official response has been issued by Pakistan's military or government.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Mindaugas Sinkevicius becomes Lithuania PM after parliament approves manifesto
item.description: Lithuania's parliament approved Mindaugas Sinkevicius' government program on Tuesday. This action allows him to formally assume the prime minister's office. The new administration will maintain high defense spending and support Ukraine. They will also continue seeking United States troop presence as a deterrent. The center-left coalition secured a narrow majority in the legislature.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: New York bans data center permits for one year: 'As data center development threatens ⁠to hike up utility bills... it's my responsibility...'
item.description: New York bans data centers: In a groundbreaking decision, New York has imposed a one-year halt on new large data center constructions. Citing environmental concerns and energy consumption, Governor Kathy Hochul has set a significant precedent across the United States. The state will evaluate the environmental implications of data centers amid rising worries regarding AI infrastructure development and its sustainability.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: 2,000 years after Pompeii vanished beneath volcanic ash in one of history's deadliest volcanic disasters, the ancient Roman street is blooming with flowers, offering a symbol of nature's renewal
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Argentina lost jaguars from its Iberá wetlands nearly 70 years ago, but conservationists have now brought the country's largest wild cat back through an ambitious rewilding project
item.description: Jaguars have returned to Argentina's Iberá wetlands after a 70-year absence. Conservationists released a female jaguar and her cubs into Gran Iberá Park. This reintroduction is part of a broader rewilding effort to restore ecosystems. The project aims to re-establish a self-sustaining jaguar population in the region. This initiative helps rebuild the wetland's ecological network and biodiversity.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Britain hunted beavers to extinction around 400 years ago, but they are now returning to England's wetlands to rebuild rivers and restore disappearing ecosystems
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Why Oklahoma speed-limit signs say “No tolerance,” and what it really means for drivers
item.description: Oklahoma's "No Tolerance" signs remind drivers that posted speed limits are absolute. These signs reinforce the state's strict approach to speeding enforcement and traffic safety. Legally, exceeding the speed limit by even one mile per hour is a violation. Law enforcement officers still use discretion in issuing citations for minor infractions. Drivers should treat posted speeds as the actual limit to avoid penalties.

item.title: NASA’s New Horizons travelled 3 billion miles over 9 years to reach Pluto – but it was moving too fast to stop when it arrived
item.description: NASA's New Horizons spacecraft completed its swift flyby of Pluto after a nine-year journey. The mission prioritized data collection over live communication during the brief encounter. Scientists received approximately 50 billion bits of data over fifteen months after the flyby. This slow data return revealed Pluto's complex and geologically active icy world. The mission highlighted the trade-off between reach and the patience required for discovery.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Why do we forget why we walked into a room? The fascinating science behind the "Doorway Effect"
item.description: Entering a new room can cause temporary memory lapses for your original intention. This doorway effect is a normal brain process for organizing experiences into distinct events. It happens as your brain updates its mental model of the environment. Working memory limitations and attention shifts also contribute to these forgetful moments. Retracing your steps often helps retrieve the forgotten thought by restoring context.
item.dc:creator: Smita Mishra

item.title: Rare fossil reveals T. rex’s attack style
item.description: An amazing fossil discovery shows a T. rex tooth stuck in an Edmontosaurus skull, dating back 66 million years. This proves a direct clash between these dinosaurs. The tooth confirms T. rex involvement, meaning the Edmontosaurus was either attacked or scavenged. This is crucial evidence for understanding T. rex's hunting habits.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Astronomers have spent decades searching nearby stars for another Earth, and now they may have found one just 25 light-years away
item.description: Scientists have found GJ 3378b, a planet near Earth with potential for life. This exoplanet is twice Earth's size and orbits within its star's habitable zone. Researchers used ground-based instruments to update measurements of GJ 3378b's mass. Future telescopes will study its atmosphere to confirm habitability and search for biosignatures. This discovery adds GJ 3378b to the list of promising nearby exoplanets.
item.dc:creator: TOI Science Desk

item.title: Marine fossils found near Mount Everest summit tell the remarkable story of a vanished ocean
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: UK seeks law change to deport Pakistani rapist and grooming gang leader Shabir Ahmed
item.description: The UK government is changing a law that prevents serious criminals' deportation. Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood announced Parliament will amend the Immigration Act 1971. This change targets individuals like grooming gang leader Shabir Ahmed. Ahmed was jailed for rape and sexual offenses against young girls. The government seeks to remove legal protections for such serious offenders.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Captain Cook's famous shipwreck RI 2394 finally found after a 26-year search in Rhode Island
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Turkey's ancient city of Ani reveals hidden Seljuk tombs that survived for centuries underground
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk

item.title: Trump tariffs struck down by US Supreme Court: How much has been refunded so far
item.description: The Supreme Court's recent decision led the US government to refund a staggering eighty-one billion dollars in tariffs, overturning many of President Donald Trump's imposed tariffs. This fiscal year has already seen a notable surge in refunds according to Treasury budget data. A ruling from the US Court of International Trade in May also challenged these blanket tariffs.
item.dc:creator: TOI World Desk
